The National Women=E2=80=99s Soccer League has been on a recent tear=E2=80=
=94locking in significant revenue increases via its new media deals, signed=
 last fall, and enjoying the bounce provided by Willow Bay and Bob Iger=E2=
=80=99s purchase of a controlling stake in Angel City FC, which values the =
franchise at $250 million. Of course, the Olympic women=E2=80=99s team gold=
 medal victory in Paris, watched by 9 million people=E2=80=94the most viewe=
rs since the Athens Games in 2004=E2=80=94further enhanced the sport=E2=80=
=99s profile.

A lot of this growth has taken place under NWSL commissioner Jessica Berman=
, who announced last week that the league had signed a new labor deal a ful=
l two years before the current one expires=E2=80=94and one that offers some=
 unusual quirks, like ending the draft. Berman, a former employment attorne=
y at Proskauer, cut her teeth in the
sports business by working on labor deals before moving in house at the NHL=
 for 13 years. I sat down with her last week to discuss the unusual and his=
toric negotiations.

Negotiating Cycles

John Ourand: Some of your team valuations, like the recent Iger-Bay deal fo=
r Angel City, have been eye-popping for me. Why are you seeing such growth =
right now?

Jessica Berman: When the average American closes their eyes and thinks of g=
reatness and soccer, they think of women. That=E2=80=99s not true in almost=
 any other circumstance, particularly in team sports. That=E2=80=99s our fo=
undation. That=E2=80=99s our backdrop. The second piece is the backdrop of =
investing in sports, generally, as an asset class. Historically, sports tea=
m valuations outperform any other asset class, and we are at the inception =
of our growth. It=E2=80=99s a good investment.

The third piece is that we have proven case studies around how our league i=
s growing. We can demonstrate that when our owners have invested in this li=
ke a business, it grows. There
aren=E2=80=99t many untapped opportunities in sports that engender the kind=
 of confidence that our league is giving investors right now. Sports is alw=
ays a supply-and-demand challenge, but we have a lot more people who want t=
o invest than we have assets to sell.

You recently signed new media deals with Amazon, CBS, ESPN, and Scripps tha=
t will bring in meaningful media rights increases for four years. Typically=
, when players see their league signing lucrative media deals, everybody wa=
nts more. Was there ever a moment where talks had the potential to blow up?

One of our goals was to establish a long-term labor peace beyond the next m=
edia-rights cycle. What you described is definitely a dynamic in sports. It=
 is also true that when you have an impending expiration, it can be a distr=
action if you=E2=80=99re looking for incremental investment from media part=
ners, owners, or sponsors. We made the decision last year to strike a short=
er-term deal with our media
partners; it expires in four years, and we=E2=80=99re midway through our fi=
rst year.

We=E2=80=99re already thinking about the strategy around our next negotiati=
on. We recognized there was a window right now for us to do this before the=
 men=E2=80=99s World Cup, before we=E2=80=99re in real negotiations and dis=
cussions and planning for our next media-rights cycle, which gives us a cha=
nce to align for the longer term so that we could unlock incremental invest=
ment.

You had two years left on your labor deal. Why the push to get it done now?

Last summer, we decided that it was the right time to invite the Players As=
sociation to the bargaining table and engage in voluntary bargaining. We kn=
ew that in order to build the league for the future, we needed to deconstru=
ct various aspects of the collective bargaining agreement, and we knew that=
 we could do it proactively, or wait until we got to the end of the agreeme=
nt. We decided that it was worth a shot to see if we could
bring the union under the hood.

It was sufficiently in advance that it was a safe space to have an open and=
 honest discussion and negotiation, knowing that if either of us ever felt =
like it wasn=E2=80=99t constructive or getting us to the right place, we co=
uld walk away because we=E2=80=99d have plenty of time to reset bargaining =
before the 2026 expiration.

What are you happiest about in terms of the deal?

Even though I came from men=E2=80=99s sports and I respect those institutio=
ns tremendously, it=E2=80=99s also true that not every single thing that th=
ey do will make sense for our business. Given that we were in a growth mind=
set, we wanted to take the approach that we were going to pressure-test the=
 assumptions and the status quo. We asked ourselves, Does this serve us? Sh=
ould we not do anything new just because it=E2=80=99s the way it's always b=
een done?

The decision to abolish the draft and grant players full free agency is pro=
bably the
boldest example of that. We made the decision as a league, and with the sup=
port of our board, that those constructs don=E2=80=99t make sense for our b=
usiness. And it was specifically based on our unique circumstances, specifi=
cally based on the culture of soccer, and the fact that we compete in a glo=
bal labor market for talent.

More on the Draft=E2=80=A6

Who pushed for that, the owners or the players?

Both. We collectively agreed that this was in the best interest of the leag=
ue. In order to manifest our mission of being the best league in the world,=
 and attracting, developing, and retaining the best talent, the old mechani=
sm for how players enter our league no longer serves our interest.

I=E2=80=99m proud of our approach to innovation and being a positive disrup=
tor in our industry, and unlocking things that might=E2=80=99ve been unimag=
inable in more traditional, established, longer-standing leagues, with long=
er operations, that have been around for a hundred-plus years. We can do th=
ings differently. And this is a really good example of that.

What are the other novel points that you are proud of?

We have the ability now to make our schedule strategically. In our
prior C.B.A., we had parameters and restrictions on when we could end our s=
eason and how many games we could play. While those things might seem tacti=
cal, they=E2=80=99re important to building the revenue engine that=E2=80=99=
s going to support the growth of this league, particularly as we look at th=
e calendar and figure out the ways that we=E2=80=99re going to create tentp=
ole events. How are we going to capture the attention of the 9 million peop=
le who watched the gold medal game at the Olympics?

You also have expansion on the horizon.

It will help in the process of selling Team 16, which is going to enter the=
 league in 2026. That would=E2=80=99ve been the year that our C.B.A. was ex=
piring. You can imagine that any uncertainty around labor peace would be ch=
allenging for the entire league, for our clubs, and especially for a team t=
hat=E2=80=99s launching. We have ongoing conversations with many bidders, a=
nd we think this offers predictability for an investor coming in the door
who=E2=80=99s looking to imagine how they=E2=80=99re going to launch a team=
 and build a business.

What=E2=80=99s the expansion timetable?

We expect to announce it by the end of this year. We=E2=80=99ll see how the=
 next few months go. We=E2=80=99re definitely on track.

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">The National Women=
=E2=80=99s Soccer League has been on a recent tear=E2=80=94locking in signi=
ficant revenue increases via its new media deals, signed last fall, and enj=
oying the bounce provided by <b>Willow Bay</b> and <b>Bob Iger</b>=E2=80=99=
s purchase of a controlling stake in Angel City FC, which values the franch=
ise at $250 million. Of course, the Olympic women=E2=80=99s team gold medal=
 victory in Paris, watched by 9 million people=E2=80=94the most viewers sin=
ce the Athens Games in 2004=E2=80=94further enhanced the sport=E2=80=99s pr=
ofile.<br/><br/>A lot of this growth has taken place under NWSL commissione=
r <b>Jessica Berman</b>, who announced last week that the league had signed=
 a new labor deal a full <i>two years</i> before the current one expires=E2=
=80=94and one that offers some unusual quirks, like ending the draft. Berma=
n, a former employment attorney at Proskauer, cut her teeth in the
sports business by working on labor deals before moving in house at the NHL=
 for 13 years. I sat down with her last week to discuss the unusual and his=
toric negotiations.<br/></td>
         </tr>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;"><b><i>John Ourand</=
i>: Some of your team valuations, like the recent Iger-Bay deal for Angel C=
ity, have been eye-popping for me. Why are you seeing such growth right now=
?</b><br/><br/><i>Jessica Berman</i>: When the average American closes thei=
r eyes and thinks of greatness and soccer, they think of women. That=E2=80=
=99s not true in almost any other circumstance, particularly in team sports=
=2E That=E2=80=99s our foundation. That=
=E2=80=99s our backdrop. The second pi=
ece is the backdrop of investing in sports, generally, as an asset class. H=
istorically, sports team valuations outperform any other asset class, and w=
e are at the inception of our growth. It=E2=80=99s a good investment.<br/><=
br/>The third piece is that we have proven case studies around how our leag=
ue is growing. We can demonstrate that when our owners have invested in thi=
s like a business, it grows. There
aren=E2=80=99t many untapped opportunities in sports that engender the kind=
 of confidence that our league is giving investors right now. Sports is alw=
ays a supply-and-demand challenge, but we have a lot more people who want t=
o invest than we have assets to sell. <br/><br/><b>You recently signed new =
media deals with Amazon, CBS, ESPN, and Scripps that will bring in meaningf=
ul media rights increases for four years. Typically, when players see their=
 league signing lucrative media deals, everybody wants more. Was there ever=
 a moment where talks had the potential to blow up?</b><br/><br/>One of our=
 goals was to establish a long-term labor peace beyond the next media-right=
s cycle. What you described is definitely a dynamic in sports. It is also t=
rue that when you have an impending expiration, it can be a distraction if =
you=E2=80=99re looking for incremental investment from media partners, owne=
rs, or sponsors. We made the decision last year to strike a shorter-term de=
al with our media
partners; it expires in four years, and we=E2=80=99re midway through our fi=
rst year. <br/><br/>We=E2=80=99re already thinking about the strategy aroun=
d our next negotiation. We recognized there was a window right now for us t=
o do this before the men=E2=80=99s World Cup, before we=E2=80=99re in real =
negotiations and discussions and planning for our next media-rights cycle, =
which gives us a chance to align for the longer term so that we could unloc=
k incremental investment.<br/><br/><b>You had two years left on your labor =
deal. Why the push to get it done now?</b><br/><br/>Last summer, we decided=
 that it was the right time to invite the Players Association to the bargai=
ning table and engage in voluntary bargaining. We knew that in order to bui=
ld the league for the future, we needed to deconstruct various aspects of t=
he collective bargaining agreement, and we knew that we could do it proacti=
vely, or wait until we got to the end of the agreement. We decided that it =
was worth a shot to see if we could
bring the union under the hood. <br/><br/>It was sufficiently in advance th=
at it was a safe space to have an open and honest discussion and negotiatio=
n, knowing that if either of us ever felt like it wasn=E2=80=99t constructi=
ve or getting us to the right place, we could walk away because we=E2=80=99=
d have plenty of time to reset bargaining before the 2026 expiration.<br/><=
br/><b>What are you happiest about in terms of the deal?</b><br/><br/>Even =
though I came from men=E2=80=99s sports and I respect those institutions tr=
emendously, it=E2=80=99s also true that not every single thing that they do=
 will make sense for our business. Given that we were in a growth mindset, =
we wanted to take the approach that we were going to pressure-test the assu=
mptions and the status quo. We asked ourselves, <i>Does this serve us?</i> =
<i>Should we not do anything new just because it=E2=80=99s the way it's=
 always been done? </i><br/><br/>The decision to abolish the draft and gran=
t players full free agency is probably the
boldest example of that. We made the decision as a league, and with the sup=
port of our board, that those constructs don=E2=80=99t make sense for our b=
usiness. And it was specifically based on our unique circumstances, specifi=
cally based on the culture of soccer, and the fact that we compete in a glo=
bal labor market for talent.<br/></td>
         </tr>

n around for a hundred-plus years. We can do things differently. And this i=
